title: Josh & Joel
subtitle: Wedding Identity & Stationery
description:
When Josh and Joel approached me for their wedding at East Riddlesden Hall, they were clear that they wanted to eschew the traditional scripts and delicate flourishes of standard stationery in favour of something bold, playful and reflective of their lifestyle.

The resulting identity puts typography centre stage. I began by creating a custom “logo” of their names, designed to be adapted across various formats and textures. For the stationery itself, I developed a kinetic typographic system where the letterforms create dynamic shapes that support the information. To ground the project in its location, I created an adaptable graphic language inspired by the architectural features of the venue, used for both iconography and on-site wayfinding. Integrated with a hand-illustrated “two stags” motif by the groom, Joel, the final suite was a tactile, high-impact celebration uniquely tailored to them.

title: Hannah Collins Garden Design
subtitle: Identity, Web Design, Stationery
description:
Hannah Collins produces stunning, bespoke contemporary gardens, yet her previous branding failed to reflect the premium, structural nature of her work. She required a visual identity that could match the calibre of her designs and elevate her presence in a competitive market.

The resulting identity is built on a flexible, typographic system that mirrors the landscape itself. By stacking the name and introducing a clean vertical "slice," the logo evokes the disciplined lines of garden borders, trellises, and pagodas. The words "Garden Design" are set in an elegant italic, placed along the "path" created by the negative space—representing the contrast between a garden's rigid architectural bones and the organic, flowing forms of its planting. The logoform is inherently adaptable; it can expand or contract to frame photography, physically interacting with images of her work to emphasise the relationship between the designer and the land.
images: images/projects/Hannah Collins/Hannah Collins Logo Full Colour.webp

title: Imps Review Magazine
subtitle: Editorial Design & Publishing
description:
Self-initiated annual magazine documenting the soul of a cycling club.

Inspired by the tactile beauty of high-end independent journals, this annual magazine launched in 2022 to document the Halifax Imperial Wheelers cycling club. Spanning 90–140 pages, these publications serve as a premium archive of the club’s journey, featuring long-form ride reports, curated photography, and deep dives into local cycling history.

Built on a rigorous modular grid, the compact A5 format provides a creative challenge: balancing dense content with impactful visual storytelling. By playing with scale and intentional white space, the layouts feel expansive and breathable despite the small scale.

As the sole editor and designer, the full production pipeline is managed end-to-end — handling everything from photography and interviews to ad sales and print management.

title: Kate & Tom
subtitle: Wedding Identity & Stationery
description:
For Katie and Tom's wedding, the brief was to blend two distinct passions: Tom's career in the horse racing industry and Katie's love for the 60s and 70s pop culture. The result is a bold, non-traditional identity that trades classic wedding tropes for a "race-day" aesthetic filtered through a technicolor lens.

The typographic direction pays homage to early pop music and the bold, expressive fonts of the late 60s, creating a fun and carefree atmosphere. This was paired with a clever "Race Card" seating plan, where guests were listed as runners on a scorecard and tables were named after iconic grounds — with their home course, York, taking pride of place as the head table.

It's a project that proves bespoke stationery is at its best when it's deeply personal, playful, and a little bit rock and roll.

title: MAMIL
subtitle: Branding & Menu Design
description:
Branding, experiential and menu design for a small chain of cycling cafes.

Approached in 2021 by Dave, a keen cyclist with a long history in the catering industry, the brief was to create a brand identity for a cafe with a cycling theme. Cycling culture is notoriously stylish and steeped in the history of famous European races — the cafe needed to reflect this while maintaining a touch of humour and self-awareness.

Working closely with the client, the brand played off the often derogatory term for cyclists: the Middle Aged Man in Lycra, or MAMIL. The logomark references the cycling connection subtly, without being exclusive to cyclists.

The menus introduced secondary fonts and colours to avoid an overly oppressive, monotone look. Wall murals utilising the handlebars motif mingled with framed jerseys and homages to icons of cycling to give each cafe a real 'clubhouse' vibe.

Mamil went on to open a further two bars, all following this same visual identity — helping them achieve success and brand recognition across Calderdale.

title: Craft Beer Maps
subtitle: Identity, Print
description:
What began as a lockdown passion project evolved into a locally-backed tourism initiative. Recognising the wealth of independent bars in Halifax, I developed a conceptual "Craft Beer Trail" to help locals and visitors navigate the town’s finest craft establishments. Inspired by the iconic New York Subway map, the identity system utilised a clear, colour-coded wayfinding language to connect disparate venues into a singular, walkable experience.

The project gained significant organic traction, eventually catching the attention of Discover Halifax. They commissioned an initial print run of 2,000 maps for local distribution; following an overwhelming response from both bars and the public, a second run of 5,000 copies was ordered a year later. The success of the Halifax trail led to a subsequent commission from a collective of independent bar owners in Huddersfield, where I adapted the system into a more "underground," grassroots aesthetic to better suit the town’s unique nightlife character.

title: St Peter’s Consort
subtitle: Identity, Posters & Socials
description:
Identity, poster design and social media for an established choir.

Since 2018, leading a visual transformation for St. Peter’s Consort, a choir with over four decades of history. Prior to the partnership, the group’s public materials lacked a cohesive brand voice. The goal was to move their image away from ‘homemade’ aesthetics toward a professional, high-impact identity that matches the calibre of their performances.

The core of this evolution was a strategic design system. A disciplined typographic template established a clear information hierarchy, ensuring the choir is instantly recognisable across the region. This framework provides the anchor for their brand while leaving generous space for bespoke imagery that captures the unique spirit of each concert.

Over the last eight years, this shift in visual professionalisation has helped redefine the Consort’s reputation — transitioning them from a small local group into a widely recognised and respected fixture of the regional music scene.
